text:
Although numerous studies have explored the trends of participation and performance in single-stages events such as Ironman Hawaii, there is little knowledge on multi-stages ultra-triathlons such as Ultraman Hawaii. This competition demands further investigation as it comprises a 515km ultra-triathlon covering 3days of contest. As such, the present study aims to determine: (1) trends of participation and performance for 3 decades of the contest, (2) men’s and women’s performance differences concerning the overall contest as well as each discipline of the contest (biking, running and swimming), and (3) when in life athletes achieve the personal record on such a contest.

Here, we present data from 98 female and 570 male runners whose finishing data were recorded over the whole 30-year period (1983–2012). The performance of the annual winners and the three best runners in each of the sexes was analysed by simple linear regression of race time to year and race time to age. While the numbers of men running the race has changed little over the period, numbers of women running have greatly increased. In addition, the performance of the top three men and women had decreased over the period, and the sex performance differential had reduced from 24.3% to 11.5%.

I've learned many things from this analysis, but perhaps most interestingly, the top speed in the swim and the cycle and the run came in along with the observation that the winning age of athletes is increasing, from 28 to 47 for men and 30 to 50 or more for women. Generally we are seeing that the median age of the top three in each sex is creeping up to 50 or thereabouts. Longevity and experience seem to be a new attribute to the description of an Ultraman Champion. The sex gap in ulra-triathlons has been shown to be decreasing steadily with time and is currently at about 12% which is similar to the gap seen at all endurance endurance milestones except that this work needs a bit more research to identify all of the new physiological and psychological properties that are unique to multi-day multi-discipline events.
explanation: The text shows some unusual phrasing, first-person statement, and minor typos/redundancies (e.g., 'I've learned', 'ulra-triathlons'), which are common in AI-generated or draft content, though the content also mirrors a plausible scientific abstract.
